title: Data Types
description: Java's eight primitive types, their sizes and default values, and the difference between primitives and object (reference) types.
objectives: List Java's eight primitive types and what each one stores
Understand default values a primitive gets when not explicitly initialized
Understand the difference between a primitive type and an object (reference) type
Use the correct literal syntax for long, float, and char values
Avoid common data type mistakes like overflow and type confusion

sections.text: 1. Why Do We Need Different Types?

In the Variables lesson, we only used int. But not every value is a whole number — some are decimals, some are single characters, some are true/false. Java needs a way to know what kind of value a variable holds, and a type is exactly that.
A data type tells the compiler what kind of value a variable can store, how much memory to reserve for it, and what operations make sense on it.
2. Primitive Types
Java has 8 built-in primitive types. A primitive type stores its value directly — not a reference to something else.
You do not need to memorize exact bit sizes right now. What matters at this stage: int and double are the two you'll use constantly; the rest exist for specific size/precision needs you'll grow into.
3. Whole Numbers: byte, short, int, long
These four types all store whole numbers (no decimal point) — they only differ in how large a number they can hold.

The L at the end of 8000000000L tells Java to treat this literal as a long. Without the L, Java assumes it's an int by default — and this particular number is too big to fit in an int, causing a compile error.

sections.title: In practice
points: int is the default choice for whole numbers — used almost everywhere in this course
long is used when a number might exceed int's range (roughly ±2.1 billion)
byte and short are rarely used in everyday code — mainly relevant for memory-constrained situations, which we won't focus on yet

sections.text: 4. Decimal Numbers: float and double

The f at the end of 65.5f tells Java to treat this literal as a float. Without the f, Java assumes any decimal literal is a double by default — so float weight = 65.5; (no f) would not compile, since you can't put a double value into a float variable without saying so explicitly.

sections.title: In practice
points: double is the default choice for decimal numbers in Java — used far more often than float
float takes half the memory but is less precise — mainly used when memory matters more than precision

sections.text: 5. Single Characters: char

A char uses single quotes ('A') and holds exactly one character. Double quotes ("A") create a String instead, which is a different type — covered in the Strings topic soon. Mixing these up is one of the most common beginner errors.
6. True or False: boolean

sections.code:
boolean isLoggedIn = true;
boolean hasPermission = false;

sections.text: A boolean can only ever be true or false — nothing else. You will use boolean constantly once we reach conditions (if statements) and loops.
7. Default Values

In the Variables lesson we said Java won't let you read a local (inside a method) variable before initializing it. But primitive fields that belong to a class (covered later, in Object-Oriented Programming) do get automatic default values if you don't set one.

sections.title: Default values (for class fields, not local variables)

sections.text: We have not learned classes and fields yet — this table is only here to prevent future confusion. Right now, inside main(), you must always initialize your own variables; the compiler won't let you skip it.
8. Primitives vs Object (Reference) Types
Every type in Java is either primitive or a reference type (also called an object type). The 8 types above are the only primitives — everything else, like String, is a reference type.
A type whose variable directly stores its value in memory (e.g. int, double, char, boolean).
A type whose variable stores a reference (an address, conceptually) pointing to an object that lives elsewhere. String, arrays, and every class are reference types.

sections.title: Box vs signpost analogy
sections.text: A primitive variable is like a box that directly holds its content — open int age and 25 is right there. A reference type variable is like a signpost pointing to a house down the road — String name doesn't hold the text itself, it points to where the actual text object lives.

We are only previewing this distinction here. Its real consequences — like how reference types behave differently when passed around, or compared with == — are studied properly once we reach Object-Oriented Programming. For now, just recognize: int, double, char, boolean = primitive. String and everything else = reference type.
9. Putting It Together

sections.output:
Aman is 25 years old.
Height: 5.9
Grade: A
Passing: true

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing a large whole number literal without the L suffix — e.g. long x = 10000000000; fails to compile without the L.
Writing a decimal literal into a float without the f suffix — e.g. float f = 3.14; fails to compile without the f.
Using double quotes for a char — 'A' is a char, "A" is a String; they are not interchangeable.
Assuming all types have a default value you can rely on inside main() — that's only true for class fields, not local variables.
Confusing float and double — double is the safe default for decimals in this course unless you have a specific reason to use float.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are Java's primitive types?
questions.answer: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean. There are exactly 8, and each stores its value directly rather than as a reference.

questions.question: What is the difference between a primitive type and a reference type?
questions.answer: A primitive type variable stores its value directly. A reference type variable stores a reference to an object that lives elsewhere in memory. String, arrays, and classes are all reference types.

questions.question: Why do you need L after a long literal, or f after a float literal?
questions.answer: Java treats whole number literals as int and decimal literals as double by default. The L or f suffix tells the compiler to treat the literal as a long or float instead, which is required when assigning to a variable of that type.

questions.question: What is the difference between char and String in Java?
questions.answer: char holds exactly one character and uses single quotes, like 'A'. String holds a sequence of characters (text) and uses double quotes, like "A", and is a reference type, not a primitive.

questions.question: What is the default value of an uninitialized boolean field?
questions.answer: false. This default applies to fields belonging to a class; local variables inside a method must always be explicitly initialized before use.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Java has 8 primitive types — byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean — each storing its value directly. Everything else, like String, is a reference type, whose variable stores a reference to an object elsewhere in memory rather than the value itself. Literals need suffixes to match certain types: L for long, f for float. Class fields get automatic default values (0, 0.0, false, etc.) if not set, but local variables inside a method must always be initialized before Java lets you read them.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Primitives
sections.text: "Bytes Sit In Long Fields, Deciding Character Booleans" — Byte, Short, Int, Long, Float, Double, Char, Boolean — the 8 primitives, in a common teaching order (small whole numbers → big whole numbers → decimals → char → boolean).

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does long big = 10000000000L; need the L?
What's the difference between char and String?
Is String a primitive type or a reference type?
